sentences.text: Yes, of course, there was--
People worked in the shop.
In the shop.
Even if you worked in a shop, you had absolutely no guarantee--
No security.
--that you will survive.
And every moment was a total-- practically ever hour, every day that you survived was a--
Miracle.
--a chance on the lottery if you survived.
There was no-- there was no security of the remotest kind whatsoever for anybody.
Of course, if you had the [GERMAN] and you were a worker, there were better chances, but--
Yes, yes.
Even if you had-- if you were legally employed and so on, the chances were better.
But even then there was-- it was not completely certain.
So we went on through in this way through August, and August was one after another horrendous blockades.

And we had horrendous stories about-- we knew about that in some cases streets were really running with blood after a blockade, because they were killing people, just shooting them to terrorize, to frighten during the blockade when they were getting people out.
On one occasion, we-- when they was apparently quiet, we went across to the house where we had just fled, where we just fled, opposite the shop.
So we left the factory.
We went across, and we thought it was a quiet day, and my mother and my aunt and decided that they want to go and see some-- go to a clinic where there was something or somebody.
Maybe my uncle was working in this clinic-- I'm not sure-- nearby.

So we went, and there were about three or four children-- my cousins-- all my cousins, myself and my cousin [GERMAN], who is slightly older, who was then 12, and another cousin, Damka, who was 16, and Jurek, a cousin who was maybe 18 or 19, and an uncle who was maybe just over 20-- my youngest uncle-- 20, 21.

And we got into this flat, and my mother and my aunt went somewhere else, probably to this clinic, so we were left, those four children-- some children and some adolescents, these young men, five of us, left in this flat.
And suddenly, without any warning, we had screams and shouting and whistles outside and a horrendous noise.
And we ran to the window, looking onto [NON-ENGLISH], and we saw already-- it all was happening in seconds practically.
They had a good practice, and they were doing it very quickly so this street was already all closed.

There were Ukrainians and police everyone, and they were coming to the-- to the yards-- courtyards-- and shouting for everybody to come immediately down, and immediately the people started going down.
And we were-- I remember that we were running like rabbits from one window to another and from one window to the window in the back.
And there were opinions among us were divided whether were we all really panic stricken and we really were in a state of shock anyway and whether to go down or not to go down.
And only this one cousin of mine, Anya, who was then 12-- she was just 12-- and she decided not to go down.
And she went and hid in a hiding place on some shelf in some cupboard in a dark corner, so to say it was a big flat.
So there was a way to find out the hiding place.
And I think I lost my head probably more than the others.
I probably run out first and then ran on the landing-- on the staircase landing-- and that was already too late to do anything, because already Ukrainian was there, and he shouted at us to go down.
So we all went down, and that means myself and two cousins of mine, who were older.
She was a girl of 16 and a boy of 18, 19, and my uncle who was still a bit older.
And we all came out to the street, and then we were there right in front of the shop.
The shop was right in front next to us but, of course, it was locked and closed, and there was not way of getting in.
And so we all had-- and it was a hot, hot day, very hot and sunny, and we all had to first to sit or even kneel, I think, on the-- on this pavement there.
And then they were-- it took a long time.
I think it probably was not as long as I remember it, but to me it was probably something like half an hour or 40 minutes.
But to me it appeared absolute eternity.
And then they shouted, to, and they surrounded everybody.
So there was a long, long column of people, maybe-- maybe thousands of people whom they managed, and they surrounded us all with Ukrainians and some dogs and so on and police and Jewish police.
Everybody had a part in it.
But maybe I didn't think I remember.

I remember Ukrainians, and then they had a long ceremony of when they were kind of selection, when they were saying people who are something who has official documents and families of Jewish police, mainly families of Jewish police and said they can go out.
And indeed some people went out but not very many.

And after about maybe 40 minutes of sitting or kneeling-- I don't remember that precisely-- sitting I think on that hot pavement, we were then finally, they told us to get up and to-- and they formed us into a-- they formed us into a proper-- how to say-- proper line, proper marching group, about four people and everybody had to go with about four people in-- walking in fours.

And I remember that when I was there, when I was-- when we were waiting for them to finally to start taking us out of there, that I was then-- that it was a feeling I had then was of fear as I never had before in my life and never after.
That was quite amazing, and I-- and I knew-- I knew, I absolutely knew that that was the end, that there was no escape, that that was practically a death march.
And I was-- and I was praying as well in such an absolutely-- with so much feeling as never before and never after, I think.

And finally after about 40 minutes, they formed us into a march, and they opened the gate, which was a barbed wire gate at the end of Schultz area, and they started driving us straight ahead and then to the right toward the umschlagplatz.

And that, what I said before about terror, was now fully there, because we had-- we were driven by those [NON-ENGLISH] or maybe they were Lats, but they were beating the people and beating them with sticks and with whips and driving with screams and shouts so that everybody was walking as fast as they could.
And the atmosphere, and I think Jurek, my cousin, who was on my side, got as well few-- a few strokes of the-- being beaten with the-- with the stick.
No, I not, I think because I was walking in the middle, and I was smaller.
So I don't think I got beaten, but my cousin was certainly.
And we were walking four of us in one row.
That means my cousins, a boy of 18 and the girl of 16 and then me, and then there was my uncle walking on the left-hand side.
And he was the oldest of us.
And there was this long street, [GERMAN], which was leading roughly in direction of the umschlagplatz, and it was those big house which were everything was closed.
The windows were closed, because no-- people were afraid to look out when it was happening, you know?
People were not even coming near the windows.
It was like we were already condemned.
We were already on the other side, so people didn't even come near the window.
Whoever was there in those houses, they didn't even come-- want to come near the street.
And they say, so there were those big houses all empty, and the gates all locked.
And we were pushed ahead, and one of those gates was, however, open.
And that gate which was open, there was a bakery there, and two big blokes, bakers, were standing-- and a Jewish policeman-- were standing blocking the entrance to the gate.
And as it happened, the chap who went-- who walked immediately after us was some judenrat man, some judenrat official, with this little boy, was his son, who was walking immediately past us.
And he knew the name of this policeman who was standing.
He probably knew most of the Jewish policeman.
He knew the name of the one who was standing there in the gate, and he shouted to him, shouted something like his name.
I think it was something like Sosnofski.
The gate opened.
Can I come in?
Something like that, you know?
And that all happened in very quickly.
And when he shouted it, the Ukrainian who was running around somewhere came and started beating him up.

And my cousin, who had very quick orientation, at the same moment jumped out of the march, out of the line of people, and he pulled his-- the other cousin, the girl, after him, and she pulled me, and we all three ran towards the gate.
It was not far, because we were after all.
We ran towards the gate, and he hit the-- one of the bakers, I think, with his head, and the baker folded up.
And we all burst into the gate.
And after us, we had a lot of screaming and shouting and more people came.
There was-- there must have been something like 20 people who burst out of the march, and they all came after us.
And somehow there was terrible chaos and shooting, but they obviously didn't have enough people leading the march to start bothering after running and finding us.
So the gate got immediately closed and locked.

And they didn't search inside the gate, inside the-- which was an absolute luck out of this world for me particularly, because what happened was that in the-- when I came into the gate, somebody who was immediately after me threw me down, and I lost the-- the hand of my cousin, and she run ahead, and they still heard the screaming came from her [GERMAN] which means Stefan.
That was [GERMAN], and she was disappearing into the yard somehow, because she was pulled probably by the other cousin.
And I got up and still-- there were still some people who burst in and somebody I was thrown down second time in the middle of this passage.
And I got up again, and there was nobody already in front of me.
And the gate was closed and, of course, my first instinct was to hide as quickly as possible, really like an animal instinct more than anything.
So I started looking for a place to hide.
So I went into the first staircase, and I tried to go down, and there was the bakery.
And people were pushing me out, because they didn't want me.
And then I went to somewhere else, and people were again pushing me out, because those people, they were probably still living there.
And they were thinking, you know, people who are already in their umschlag or on their way to umschlagplatz they were somehow already-- they were already sick with plague.
They were already-- you didn't probably-- that's probably-- so they pushed me out of the bakery and they-- and then I tried to hide in some other cellar, and I didn't manage either.
I was running around.
I was completely crazy with fear at this stage.
And there was a little cart, a kind of a-- just a cart, the horse cart belonging to the bakery, with-- with sliding doors-- wooden thing.
So in my panic, I opened the sliding door and I hid inside the cart.
There was nothing there.
It was full of bread deliveries.
It belonged to the bakery.
And I stayed there for some time.

Then after some time, I don't know how long, somebody opened it, and there was this man who was speaking to me and saying me who I was and where I came from and who was my father and if my father would give him money if I brought-- if he brought me to him, and I said of course, yes.
So he was just somebody who lived in the house.
So he took me to his flat, and he was so scared as well that he put me into the drawer of the chest of drawers.
He put me into the drawer, and he closed the lock on this drawer.
I mean, they were all completely crazy, because the Germans obviously had no time in searching this house.
And if they would search the house, how would they know that I escaped out of there, that I was not his cousin or something like that?
But how would they know that I was already on the way to umschlagplatz?
They wouldn't know.
But everybody was so scared out of their wits that they didn't know what they were doing anymore.
And I started suffocating in this drawer, because there was air.
So I started hitting on the-- on there, and he luckily heard me.
So he put me in another drawer and kept me there for another hour or so.
And finally, it must have taken not so long time, but it was all-- and finally he let me out of this drawer.
Somehow I think that the blockade must have partly been over.
It probably was over some time already, maybe not.
And he put me-- and he put me next to the-- sitting on the table next to the window.
That was on the ground floor, and I saw in front of me the courtyard.
And he brought me his ausweis, his identity card, to ask me if I thought that that was a good ausweis, you know, as if I would know.
And he saw if I thought that the Germans would honor it.
You know, everybody was-- didn't know what was going on.
There was only-- nobody knew.
But he was asking for reassurance from me.
But anyway, I was sitting there, and suddenly I saw in the attic window upstairs on the other side, my cousins.
They were looking for me.
So I jumped like a madman out of there.
I jumped out of the apartment.
I ran opposite, and they saw me as well.
So they shouted for me, and we ran up, and I ran up and I met them.
And they were hiding in the attic.
They were-- I don't know how we got to that attic.
I have some vague memory of going up.
There was a hall to the attic somehow, a trapdoor, and we went on in somebody's apartment on this-- up the stair-- up the ladder, and then we went through the trapdoor and up into the attic.
And we were up in this attic.
And somebody came from below and complained that the plaster is falling on their heads because we were walking around, and the attic was probably-- it was all a bit shabby.
And I looked out of the window of the attic, and there was a window which was to the main street, to [GERMAN].
And we saw-- and then I saw rickshaws, which we taking the SS people-- the big fat SS uniform people back to their headquarters.
They obviously-- that was already after the action.
I was seeing it from the fourth, fifth floor, you know, or sixth floor-- what do I know?
And I saw those rickshaws with those fat SS people being with opened uniforms.
They were obviously still-- already it was hot, and they were already-- the service was finished.
And they were very enjoying themselves.
They were now being driven by their slaves back to their headquarters.
And then somehow, I don't remember exactly, then we of course were still not able to leave the house.
Everything was still closed and locked.
And then must have sometimes later, the blockade was finally finished, and normal the traffic, or at least people, started walking again in the street.
And Jurek and Damka and me, my cousins and me, we went down, and we went back to the shop.
And in the shop everybody already thought-- they all thought that we were gone, because my father lost his voice.

He couldn't speak anymore, because he shouted so much, because he shouted-- he ran to the main office of Schulz and was shouting, begging that they send lorry to the umschlagplatz, because after every blockade, when they had people from the shop-- workers taken to the umschlagplatz, they would send the lorry to get them out.
Because umschlagplatz was, after all, it was a big area, a big terrain.
Sometimes people waited there for hours.
Usually they waited for hours.
Sometimes they waited for 24 hours.
Sometimes they waited for longer.
And they there.
There was a hospital there and all kinds of empty buildings or half-empty buildings and a clinic and God knows what else.
And nurses and some doctors in this hospital and, of course, Jewish police outside and the Ukrainians.
And there were-- when they were-- after the blockade, there were maybe 10,000 people in a very narrow space.
The uncle, the young uncle, whom I mentioned before, who-- he didn't escape with us.
He continued, and he actually was one of the very few people, because he a real ausweis from the shop, and they let him out.
They selected him out.
From in the umschlagplatz.
So there were about 20 people who were left, among them this uncle, and the rest of the whole lot went already in the train.
So if not this split-second decision of my cousin, that would have been it.
And so we came back to the shop and everybody thought we were gone.
And until his uncle came, and even then my father was in a state, completely broken.
And this cousin of mine who hid, she was all right.
Nothing happened to her.
So that was this--
That was a real traumatic experience.
Oh, I don't know.
I don't know.
It was-- it was absolutely-- they didn't believe that I was--
--that I was alive again.
I was-- I think we were all completely-- we were all-- it was such a trauma as this.
One can hardly imagine.
And then-- but of course, those things continued, because the blockades, although that was the only time when I was actually taken in the march.
But the blockades continued all the way to the-- through August.
On my birthday, when it was 27th of August, we couldn't-- we didn't even want to stay in the shop, because we knew that it was too-- we looked every time for a different hiding hole.
I don't know exactly--
All right?
When we looked for the different hiding hole every time, we looked for a different place every time, and we hid-- there was this cellar under the house opposite.
I think probably in [INAUDIBLE] of '49.

And I hid there, all spent on my birthday, 27th of August, all day in the cellar with my mother, with-- in the beginning candle, and then we didn't even have the candle, because maybe a candle came to the end.
Maybe we were afraid even that the candle might somehow give us up.
And that was in a cellar under some abandoned shops.
And they're abandoned-- some kind of shop where-- which was already demolished, destroyed, with things thrown all over the floor, some rubbish and rubble.
And the entrance was masked. the door was-- the trapdoor was masked under the things that were on the floor.
So we spent the whole day there.
And we listened to the screams and the Ukrainians and Germans and so on and so forth all trampling all around us and collecting the people there.
And all day in a state of complete terror.
And--
Well, I was-- that was in '42.
That was my ninth birthday.
Yes.
Tape five, yeah.
